coredata.dc:title: N, S-doped carbon quantum dot for long persistence phosphor assisted all-weather solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Riaz, Shahina
Khan, Firoz
Raza, Z.A.
Theravalappil, Rajesh
Al-Rasheidi, Masoud
Abbas, Nadir
coredata.dc:description: Traditional solar cells can only work during the daytime. The solar cells that can harvest energy in all weather conditions are favorable to solving the energy crisis and ecological issues. We present a novel quantum dot-sensitized all-weather solar cell (AW-SC) that can harvest energy in both day and night conditions. The AW-SC consists of a TiO2/long persistence phosphor (LPP) photoanode sensitized by nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) and a Pt counter anode. The N and S doping reduces the band gap of CQDs and enhances the charge injection into the TiO2 layer, resulting in better solar cell performance. The LPP enables the AW-SC to store unabsorbed light from visible to infrared wavelengths and emit green fluorescence to excite the N, S-CQDs in the dark. The optimal AW-SC achieves a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 18.7% in the dark.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
All-weather solar cell
Green fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of N, S-CQDs. Low magnification (a) and high magnification (b).
FTIR spectrum of CQDs and N, S-CQDs.
Optical characteristics of CQDs and N, S-CQDs. UV–Vis (a) PL (b).
XPS analysis of N, S-CQDs. C1s (a) O1s (b) N1s (c) S2p (d).
Assembly of all-weather solar cell (a) photoluminescence images of N, S-CQDs sensitized TiO2/LPP photoanode (b).
Transmittance spectra (a) PL emission spectra of N, S-CQDs sensitized FTO/TiO2/LPP photoanode (b).C.

J–V curves for All-weather solar cells. Under simulated light irradiation (a). Under dark conditions (b). IPCE spectra of representative devices (c). Energy level distribution and charge transfer within a photoanode (d).
After glow intensity of TiO2LPP photoanode (a) Dark Jsc (b) Dark Voc (c) and FF (d) of Device-B.
Representative J–V curves of Device-B as different decay times.
Measured photovoltaic parameters of prepared devices under 100 mW cm−2 light intensity.
Measured photovoltaic parameters of prepared devices in completely dark environment. Pin = 57.8 μW cm−2.
Comparison of measured photovoltaic parameters of devices under completely dark environment with the published literature.

head.ce:title: N, S-doped carbon quantum dot for long persistence phosphor assisted all-weather solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Teacher, School of Aeronautical, Automotive, Chemical and Materials Engineering, Loughborough University, Leicestershire, LE11 3TU, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Aeronautical, Automotive, Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University Teacher, School of Aeronautical, Automotive, Chemical and Materials Engineering, Loughborough University, Leicestershire, LE11 3TU, United Kingdom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional solar cells can only work during the daytime. The solar cells that can harvest energy in all weather conditions are favorable to solving the energy crisis and ecological issues. We present a novel quantum dot-sensitized all-weather solar cell (AW-SC) that can harvest energy in both day and night conditions. The AW-SC consists of a TiO2/long persistence phosphor (LPP) photoanode sensitized by nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) and a Pt counter anode. The N and S doping reduces the band gap of CQDs and enhances the charge injection into the TiO2 layer, resulting in better solar cell performance. The LPP enables the AW-SC to store unabsorbed light from visible to infrared wavelengths and emit green fluorescence to excite the N, S-CQDs in the dark. The optimal AW-SC achieves a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 18.7% in the dark.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy is receiving significant attention as one of the most promising renewable and clean energy [1,2]. To date, all kinds of state-of-the-art solar cells show a persistent rise in power conversion efficiency (PCE) via innovation in material and technology optimization [3,4]. However, it is still challenging for all modern solar cells to generate electricity under dark conditions. Sunlight illumination is the prerequisite for these modern solar cells to offer photons to be absorbed by photosensitizers [5–7]. Therefore, the photovoltaic (PV) revolution's constant goal is to create all-weather solar cells (AW-SCs), which can produce electricity in light and darkness.

Lead (Pb)-based devices currently dominate the quantum dot (QD) PV research. Several researchers have reported PCEs exceeding 16% for cells based on these materials [8–10]. The benefit of QD-PVs is that they can more effectively capture solar energy by taking advantage of QD-like behavior; as a result, the unique QD features must be kept while permitting efficient charge transport [11]. Alternative QDs for usage in eco-friendly PVs must be thoroughly researched due to the unfavorable toxicity of Pb compounds, as was previously indicated [12]. In order to sensitize TiO2, carbon-based QDs have been employed in PVs in conjunction with polymers. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have never been utilized as the only absorbent layer. C-based materials also have minimal toxicity and can be used with environmentally friendly methods [13,14].

CQDs have attracted increased research attention as the light absorber in PV due to their excellent performance in photon absorption and electron excitation [15–17]. CQDs-based solar cells are the popular cost-effective energy harvesters [18–23]. Even though many studies have focused on using undoped or nitrogen-doped CQDs for solar cell applications. Few studies reported the application of N and sulfur-doped CQDs in solar cells [17,18,23–25]. The addition of the S atom will enhance the capability of CQDs to donate electrons [26–31].

Recently CQDs-sensitized AW-SCs proposed by Q. Tang and his group have provided a promising approach to producing electricity in any weather. This group successfully fabricated undoped CQD-sensitized AW-SCs that yielded a PCE of 15.1% in dark and 0.17% under sunlight illumination [21,32,33]. Motivated by this line of thought, we synthesize N and S co-doped CQDs (N, S-CQDs) to fabricate AW-SCs. Ascorbic acid and ammonium persulfate were used as C and N and S sources for the N, S-CQDs respectively. The AW-SCs were fabricated, which comprised N, S-CQDs and green-emitting LPPs combined with mesoscopic TiO2 to enhance the photoelectric performance of the device under dark-light conditions. By doping CQDs with N and S, the bandgap of CQDs is reduced and the charge injection into the TiO2 layer is enhanced, which improves the photovoltaic performance of the device. The LPP layer acts as a light storage material that can capture and store the incident light when the illumination is strong and release strong visible light when the illumination is weak or dark. The TiO2/LPP photoanode emits green luminescence that continuously excites the electrons in N, S-CQDs, enabling the device to generate electricity under dark-light conditions.

N, S-CQDs were synthesized by following the protocol given in Ref. [34]. The synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 . Briefly, 0.88 g of ascorbic acid has been dissolved in 40 ml of deionized water, to 2.282 g of N, S- dopant, ammonium persulfate (APS) was added. The whole mixture was transferred to stainless steel autoclave and allowed to react at 220 °C for 24 h. After that, the mixture was filtered via a 0.22 μm filter paper. The obtained N, S-CQDs were dialyzed (dialyzed bag, MWCO 1000 Da) for 12 h. The final product was freeze-dried for further use. We synthesize undoped-CQDs using a previously described procedure [35].

The TiO2 colloid was synthesized using the method described in a previous study [36]. A clean FTO glass with an active cell area of 0.25 cm2 was coated with the TiO2 colloid by doctor-blade technique to form a film with a thickness of about 10 μm. The TiO2 film underwent calcination at 450 °C for 30 min. After that, the m-TiO2 films were submerged in concentrated CQDs or N, S-CQDs solutions for 24 h to obtain CQDs or N, S-CQDs-sensitized m-TiO2 photoanodes. The calculated N, S-CQDs loading was ∼1 mg cm−2 . The green LPP (Lot # MKCJ7191, Sigma Aldrich) was dispersed in acetonitrile (0.25 mg ml−1) and spin-coated (at 2000 rpm for 30 s) onto the CQDs or N, S-CQDs-sensitized m-TiO2 photoanodes to obtain m-TiO2/CQDs/LPP m-TiO2/N, S-CQDs/LPP photoanodes.

The solar cell devices were prepared in which the I−/I3 − redox electrolyte (Iodolyte Z-50, Solaronix) solution was sandwiched between a Pt counter electrode and m-TiO2/N, S-CQDs/LLP photoanode, and the devices were sealed with a Surlyn film. The devices were named depending on the type of photoanode, such as Device-A (m-TiO2/CQDs/LLP photoanode) and Device-B (m-TiO2/N, S-CQDs/LLP photoanode), respectively. For comparison, we also prepared solar cell devices without LPP, and they were named Device-C and Device-D depending on the type of photoanodes such as m-TiO2/N, S-CQDs, and m-TiO2/CQDs, respectively. The solar assembly is shown in Fig. 6a.

The current–voltage was monitored by a potentiostat electrochemical workstation under a 100 mW cm−2 light source. A standard silicon solar cell was used to adjust the light intensity. Experimental mistakes were minimized by using different samples for each of the 10 J–V curve measurements. The devices were exposed to simulated sunlight for a minute before being transferred to a dark room for the dark J–V curve measurement. Eq (1) was used to measure how much light the TiO2/LPP films emitted. Then we used silicon solar cells to measure this light intensity and compare it with the dark PV measurements.
(1)
P C E =
P max
/
P
i n

P max and P
in are maximum power input and fluorescence intensity of FTO/TiO2/LPP photoanode respectively. The measured Pin was 57.8 W cm−2. The details of all the performed characterization of samples and devices are given in Supporting information section 1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out to determine the morphology of the prepared N, S-CQDs. Fig. 2 a and b show the low and high magnification TEM images of the N, S-CQDs respectively. The size distribution measurements reveal that the N, S-CQDs have an average diameter of 3.7 ± 0.04 nm, with most of them being monodispersed and some potentially forming agglomerates. FTIR spectra of undoped CQDs and N, S-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 . In the FTIR spectrum of CQDs, characteristic peaks at 3280 cm−1, 2938 cm−1, and 1646 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H, alkyl groups and CO stretching vibrations. Comparatively, the spectrum of N, S-CQDs exhibited several special bands other than O–H, alkyl groups, and CO. The characteristics peak at 1201 cm−1 correspond to the C–N and partly C–S stretching vibrations [37]. The absorption peak at 2109 cm−1 can be attributed to the S–H stretching vibrations. Further, the CC/CN and SO vibrations confirms the N and S doping into the carbon framework [38]. The optical characteristics such as UV visible spectrum and PL spectrum of N, S-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in the UV–Vis spectrum, compared to undoped CQDs, N, S-CQDs showed a peak shift from 312 nm to 334 nm; the characteristic absorption peak appearing at 334 nm can be attributed to n = π∗ transitions of CN and CO in the N, S-CQDs [39]. In Fig. 4b the fluorescence emission spectra of undoped CQDs and N, S-CQDs under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm are shown. The undoped CQDs and N, S-CQDs displayed emission peaks at 432 nm and 437 nm, respectively. Not only the fluorescence emission intensity of N, S-CQDs is stronger compared to undoped CQDs, but also the blue shift of the maximum fluorescence emission peak of N, S-CQDs happened. Hence Blue fluorescence is indicated by the emission peak, as shown in the inset of Fig. 4b [40].

XPS analysis was carried out to further study the detailed chemical states of C, N, O, and S in N, S-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 , C1s display four prominent peaks at 284.27 eV, 284.96, 285.83 eV, and 288.58 eV, which could be ascribed to C–C/CN, C–C/CC, C–O, and CO/CN respectively, demonstrating the existence of heteroatoms in N, S-CQDs [41]. For O1s, the spectrum is resolved into two peaks centered at 531.2 eV and 532.87 eV. These peaks correspond to CO and C–OH/C–O–C in chemical states. Further, in the N1s spectrum, the peaks at 399.42 eV, 400.71 eV, and 401.49 eV correspond to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and N–H chemical states, respectively, confirming the doping of N atom in N, S-CQDs [27]. The S2p spectrum has three peaks with center energies of 168.03 eV (SO), 168.41 eV (C–SO2), and 169.35 eV (SO4), respectively, showing the successful doping of S atoms in N, S-CQs via sulfonate state [42].

Fig. 6 a shows the assembly of AW-SCs comprising of N, S-CQDs/TiO2/LPP photoanode, Pt counter electrode, and an I−/I3− redox electrolyte. Under sunlight illumination, N, S-CQDs absorb and convert incident light into electrons which enter the CB of TiO2. The transmittance spectra of FTO/TiO2, FTO, CQDs, N, S-CQDs, and LPP-loaded photoanodes are shown in Fig. 7 a. As seen in Fig. 7a, FTO/TiO2 photoanodes supported with CQDs alone or CQDs/LPP showed higher transmittance at 650 nm compared to the N, S-CQDs or N, S-CQDs/LPP supported photoanodes. The transmittance of FTO/TiO2/N, S-CQDs was 48.86% at 650 nm. When LPP was loaded to the photoanode, the optical transmittance was 16.78%. After introducing LPP into the photoanode, the significant reduction in transmittance indicates that the unabsorbed light was stored in light placeholder LPP for green emission and photoexcitation during dark conditions. The LPPs absorb and convert light into green fluorescence at λ > 385 nm. Hence, it is required to check the sensitivity of N, S-CQDs at night. Therefore, the PL emission spectra of FTO/TiO2/N, S-CQDs//LPP photoanodes were recorded. As shown in Fig. 7b, the FTO/TiO2/N, S-CQDs/LPP photoanode showed broad emission peaks at ∼500–540 nm, which signals the green-light emission from LPP which the FTO/TiO2/N, S-CQDs reabsorb.

The representative light and dark current–voltage (J–V) curves of all the prepared solar cell devices and the relative energy band alignment of TiO2 and N, S-CQDs are shown in Fig. 8 . The measured PV parameters are provided in Tables 1 and 2
. As seen, in Fig. 8a, under simulated sunlight irradiation (100 mW/cm−2), Device-D yields a short-circuit current (Jsc) of 0.86 mA/cm−2, an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.61 V, fill factor (FF) of 0.51, and the final PCE of 0.27%. The PCE was improved by replacing undoped CQDs with the N and S co-doped CQDs. Device-C showed Jsc of 1.65, Voc of 0.76 V, FF of 0.75, and PCE of 0.93%. Compared to the stat-of-the-art solar cells, the measured PCE of the optimized device is still very low, but they are still higher than those in the previous reports for CQDs solar cells [21,43,44]. As shown in Table 2, after the removal of simulated light, both the Voc and the Jsc reached zero; ultimately, the final PCEs are also zero. After introducing the LPP to the FTO/TiO2/CQDs (Device-A) and FTO/TiO2/N, S-CQDs photoanodes (Device-B), the PCEs of the devices were further improved. Device-A and Device-B showed maximum PCEs of 0.91% and 1.21%, respectively, under simulated light irradiation. However, under dark conditions, the current–voltage curves are not passing the coordinates origin, and according to the standard PCE calculation method;
(2)
P C E =
P max
/
P
i n
× 100 % =
(
J
s c
×
V
o c
× F F )
/
P
i n
× 100 %

The final PCEs of the devices were remarkably increased to 13.6% and 18.7% for Device-A and Device-B, respectively. The statistical distribution of PECs of the devices is given in Figs. S1 and S2. Hence, the photoelectric conversion under dark conditions could be attributed to solar energy storage as unabsorbed incident light and green emission at night. As shown in Fig. 8d, the N, S-CQDs can capture photons and the electrons jump from LUMO (−3.90 eV) to the CB of TiO2 (4.32 eV) and finally to the external circuit. The holes move from HOMO (−5.20 eV) to the redox potential of I−/I−3 (−4.89 eV). The high PCEs of AW-SCs in dark conditions can be attributed to the emission of monochromatic green fluorescence from LPP for CQDs illumination. The primary mechanism behind this superior performance of prepared AW-SCs was the reabsorption of unabsorbed incident light by LPP with subsequent emission, allowing for persistent irradiation of N, S-CQDs, and electricity generation during dark environments. In addition, Incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra were recorded to further confirm the light-harvesting ability of photoanodes. Fig. 8c displays the IPCE spectra of best-performing devices, Device-A and Device-B. It is seen in the spectra that the IPCE values for N, S-CQDs based solar cell device are much higher compared to the CQDs based device. The idea of CQDs-sensitized AW-SCs was first proposed by Q. Tang and his group [21,32,33]. They reported undoped CQDs-sensitized AW-SCs. Upon comparison with the literature data of the Q. Tang group and one of our previously reported studies (CQDs-embedded O, S-g-C3N4 nanotubes sensitized AW-SCs), it is revealed that N, S-CQDs sensitized AW-SCs prepared in this study showed the highest PCE under dark conditions than reported by the Q. Tang group and our own previous report (Table 3 ). To determine the existence states of CQDs and N, S-CQDs in the TiO2 film, TEM and SEM images were taken for the CQDs and N, S-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 films. It can be seen in both SEM and TEM images (Fig. S3) that TiO2 films are coated with well-distributed N, S-CQDs, which facilitate the electron transfer from the CB of QDs towards TiO2. In addition, the film pores are not blocked because of the QDs, which confirms that the electrolyte penetrates smoothly into the photoanode and reduces the oxidized QDs [45]. In contrast, CQDs-sensitized TiO2 film shows some agglomerations, which results in poor performance of the solar cell device compared to the N, SCQDs-sensitized device.

Both PCE and solar cell stability are crucial. The PL photos of the N, S-CQDs-TiO2/LPP photoanodes were taken after they had been illuminated for 2 min since the dark PCE depended on the persistent green fluorescence from LPPs. Fig. 6b displays the PL images at various decay times, and Fig. 10
displays the corresponding data over a 2-h period. In Fig. 6b, the inner dim rectangle represents N, S-CQDs/TiO2/LPP due to the light absorption by N, S-CQDs. In contrast, the outer rectangle shows green emitting LPP. As shown in Fig. 9, the measured P
in for the unsensitized TiO2/LPP photoanode is 57.8 μW/cm−2. After 100 s, the fluorescence intensity was reduced to 5.4 μW/cm−2. Device-B's Jsc, Voc, and FF were reduced from 34.7 μA/cm−2, 0.43 V, and 0.729 to 6.507 μA/cm−2, 0.124 V, and 0.323 after 2 h of decay time, respectively. Moreover, the hysteresis index (HI) of Device-A and Device-B was recorded by measuring the J–V curves in reverse and forward sweep (Fig. S4). HI was measured using Eq. (3) [46].
(3)
(
P C E
r e v e r s e
−
P C E
f o r w a r d
P C E
r e v e r s e
)
× 100 %

Compared to the control (Device-A) with an HI value of 11.24%, the N, S-doped solar cell device (Device-B) exhibited an HI value of 5.64%. The decreased HI value for Device-B can be ascribed to the lower density of defects and trap sites at the interface. Also, better charge transfer in Device-B can contribute to the decreased HI [46,47].

The persistent emission of green fluorescence is the prerequisite for AW-SCs; notably, the prepared TiO2/LPP photoanode can emit green fluorescence for 5 h at a relatively low intensity. Thus, high-intensity long-time green emission of LPP is a prerequisite for higher power generation under a dark atmosphere. However, the successful commercialization of these AW-SCs is still a significant challenge. There is still a need to introduce high optical energy capacity LPPs or the energy supply from the environment for designing high-efficiency AW-SCs in the future.

In conclusion, AW-SCs that can produce electricity in light and dark conditions are prepared by integrating LPP with the N, S-CQDs-sensitized solar cells. The AW-SC generated a PCE as high as 18.7% and a maximized Jsc of 34.7 μA, and a Voc of 0.43 V in completely dark conditions. The prepared CQD-based AW-SCs were able to generate electricity for hours. The concept of nitrogen and sulfur doping narrows the band gap of CQDs that facilitate the charge carrier's transfer in TiO2, ultimately improving the photovoltaic performance of the AW-SC device. The AW-SC device (Device-A) with undoped CQDs as an absorbing layer yielded a Jsc of 37.3 μA and a Voc of 0.32 V. While Device-B with N, S-CQDs as absorbing layers has yielded Jsc of 34.7 μA and Voc of 0.43 V. The present work is still far from optimization, but it opens the door to exploring more energy harvesting beyond sunny days.
Shahina Riaz: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software.
Firoz Khan: Data curation.
Masoud Al-Rasheidi: Writing – Original draft preparation.
Zulfiqar Ali Raza: Writing – Original draft preparation.
Rajesh Theravalappil: Visualization, Investigation.
Nadir Abbas: Supervision, Writing – Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
